coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-containing poly(β-cyclodextrin) for simultaneous removal and detection of metal ions from water
coredata.dc:creator: Silva, Emilly C.
Gomes, Charlie G.
Pina, João
Pereira, Rui F.P.
Murtinho, Dina
Fajardo, André R.
Valente, Artur J.M.
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the synthesis and characterization of supramolecular composites composed of poly(β-cyclodextrin-co-citric acid) and carbon quantum dots (QDs). These composites serve a dual purpose as adsorbents and photoluminescent probes for divalent metal ions, including Ni(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II), which can have detrimental effects on the environment. Various characterization techniques were employed to confirm the successful synthesis of the composites and the interaction between cyclodextrins and QDs. By using mathematical tools, optimal conditions for metal adsorption were determined, resulting in the composites exhibiting high adsorption capacities, reaching 220 mg/g, and impressive removal efficiencies exceeding 90 % for Ni(II) and Cu(II). The supramolecular composites also exhibit selective adsorption of metal ions with small ionic radio and can be reused with minimal loss of efficiency. In addition to their adsorption capabilities, these composites display luminescence quenching upon the adsorption of metal ions, which can be utilized for sensing applications. Spectroscopic evaluation reveals Stern-Volmer quenching constants for the accessible fraction of QDs in the range of 3777 to 13,359 M−1. The high stability of QDs on the composites allows for long-term storage. In summary, this original supramolecular composite shows promise for simultaneously monitoring and treating water and wastewater, making it a valuable tool in environmental applications.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-containing poly(β-cyclodextrin) for simultaneous removal and detection of metal ions from water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the synthesis and characterization of supramolecular composites composed of poly(β-cyclodextrin-co-citric acid) and carbon quantum dots (QDs). These composites serve a dual purpose as adsorbents and photoluminescent probes for divalent metal ions, including Ni(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II), which can have detrimental effects on the environment. Various characterization techniques were employed to confirm the successful synthesis of the composites and the interaction between cyclodextrins and QDs. By using mathematical tools, optimal conditions for metal adsorption were determined, resulting in the composites exhibiting high adsorption capacities, reaching 220 mg/g, and impressive removal efficiencies exceeding 90 % for Ni(II) and Cu(II). The supramolecular composites also exhibit selective adsorption of metal ions with small ionic radio and can be reused with minimal loss of efficiency. In addition to their adsorption capabilities, these composites display luminescence quenching upon the adsorption of metal ions, which can be utilized for sensing applications. Spectroscopic evaluation reveals Stern-Volmer quenching constants for the accessible fraction of QDs in the range of 3777 to 13,359 M−1. The high stability of QDs on the composites allows for long-term storage. In summary, this original supramolecular composite shows promise for simultaneously monitoring and treating water and wastewater, making it a valuable tool in environmental applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The waste dumped by industries is rich in organic and inorganic pollutants (Gaur et al., 2020), among which heavy metals can be highlighted, as they exhibit toxic properties and are highly harmful to living beings. Species of greatest concern include As, Pb, Hg, Cu, Cd, Cr, Ni, Mg, among others (Vareda et al., 2019; Vizuete et al., 2018). These species can come from dyes, fertilizers, pesticides, bleaching agents, and some metal alloys (Alengebawy et al., 2021). Legislation in some countries has become stricter regarding the permitted limit of these metals in wastewater, but this is not a global reality (Kinuthia et al., 2020; Vareda et al., 2019). Currently, it is possible to observe the application of several processes for the removal of heavy metals such as chemical oxidation, nanofiltration, reverse osmosis, and adsorption (Barakat, 2011; Qasem et al., 2021). Among these methods, adsorption becomes the most advantageous because it does not produce by-products, the adsorbent materials have, in general, a high rate of reuse and reapplication, profitable operating conditions, and high efficiency. Adsorption processes play a key role in the development of remediation methods once they can be easily adapted to different systems, as many materials and media can be tested for different pollutants (Roy et al., 2011; Sharma et al., 2021). No less important is the detection and monitoring of those ions, allowing the prevention of environmental damage in soils and aquatic media (Wang et al., 2022). Traditionally, the quantification of metal ions is time-consuming and involves expensive techniques such as Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectrometry (ICP OES) and Flame Atomic Absorption Spectrometry (FAAS). Thus, it becomes a priority to develop methods and probes for real-time detection of those analytes, making it possible to save costs and work (Wu et al., 2019). To optimize the adsorption capacity, selectivity, and removal of heavy metals from water, several adsorbents have been reported, including carbon nanotubes, clay minerals, and polysaccharides (Badsha et al., 2021; Cui et al., 2023; Czikkely et al., 2018; Ferreira et al., 2023; Vizuete et al., 2018). In this current scenario, materials for the simultaneous detection and removal of metal ions are needed.

Carbon quantum dots (QDs) appear as good candidates as a fluorescent platform to carry out chemical sensing (Wang et al., 2022). The abundance of cheap raw materials, the diversity of synthetic methods available, and the good photoluminescence properties are some of the advantages of QDs over other carbon materials. QDs are also considered excellent alternatives to semiconductor quantum dots due to their low toxicity, biocompatibility, and modulable fluorescence (Peng et al., 2020). Several studies in the literature classify quantum dots based on their toxicity, and a notable example is the work by Liu and Tang (2020). Their extensive literature review highlights that the degree of biocompatibility of quantum dots depends on the matrix in which they are prepared. Notably, quantum dots tend to exhibit improved biocompatibility when synthesized with compounds such as PEG, gelatin, and maltodextrin. These compounds play a crucial role in enhancing the biocompatibility of quantum dots, as supported by various studies (Ju et al., 2013; Liu & Tang, 2020). These properties make them suitable for application in bioimaging (Zhao, Chen, et al., 2015), sensors (Gao et al., 2016), catalysis (Fernando et al., 2015), adsorbents, and optoelectronic devices. As they are obtained from different sources, they can have moldable functional groups for the adsorption of different species, thus resulting in a material that, in addition to adsorptive capabilities, allows the visual detection of metallic species by modifying its luminescent properties.

The assembly of QDs in other materials has been explored in the literature mainly for sensing applications. These nanosized materials can be immobilized on binders that provide stability, and support, preserving their luminescent properties (Peng et al., 2020). Among the possibilities in terms of supporting materials, QDs functionalized in β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) can be an attractive material due to its amphiphilic properties and ability to form host-guest supramolecular adducts (Luo et al., 2013). This characteristic of cyclodextrins is also advantageous in the adsorption of metals, as described in some works in the literature (Zhao, Repo, et al., 2015, Zhao et al., 2017). Based on this, in this study, we describe the preparation of a β-cyclodextrin polymer functionalized with QDs. These functionalized QDs were utilized for the adsorption and luminescent detection of the following divalent ions: Cd(II), Cu(II), Ni(II), and Pb(II) in an aqueous medium. We hypothesized that the combination of β-CD and QDs could create a bi-functional material capable of simultaneously detecting and removing hazardous metal ions from water, works recognized in the literature as (Ghoul et al., 2010) bring important aspects of the composition and reticulation of β-cyclodextrin. This work presents novel aspects when composing cyclodextrins with quantum dots for adsorption and sensing of quantum dots. It is not a simple task to find research that studies the hosting capacity of cyclodextrins applied as probes in environmental issues. To determine the best experimental conditions, we conducted a series of experiments and surface response analyses. Additionally, we reported and discussed the effect of counter-ions and performed a selectivity analysis of the sorption process.

β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD, >98 %) was purchased from Acros Organics (USA). Citric acid (CA) from Labchem Inc. (USA). Polyethylene glycol (PEG-400, P.A., Mw = 400 g/mol) from Êxodo Científica (Brazil). Potassium dihydrogen phosphate, nickel(II) nitrate hexahydrate (>96 %), cadmium(II) nitrate tetrahydrate (>99 %), and cadmium(II) acetate dihydrate (99.8 %) from Riedel-de Haën (Germany). Copper(II) acetate monohydrate (98 %) and lead(II) acetate trihydrate (99 %) from J. T. Baker (USA). Copper(II) nitrate (99 %) from Chem-lab (Belgium). Copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate (>98.5 %) from M&B Laboratory Chemicals (UK). Nickel(II) sulfate heptahydrate (97 %) and cadmium sulfate octahydrate (99 %) from Merck (Germany). Nickel(II) acetate tetrahydrate (98 %) and sodium nitrate (99 %) from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Lead(II) sulfate (99.8 %) from BDH Chemicals (India) and lead(II) nitrate (99 %) from Honeywell (USA). All chemicals were used as received. Standard solutions, 1000 mg/L, of each metal ion (Cu(II), Ni(II), Cd(II), or Pb(II)) in 0.5 mol/L nitric acid for FAAS (PanReac AppliChem, Germany) were used for AAS calibration. All solutions were prepared by using Milli-Q water.

The synthesis of quantum dots (QDs) was adapted from Fan et al. (2014). Initially, 5 mL of PEG-400 was dissolved in 45 mL of deionized water in a beaker and subsequently transferred to a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated to 120 °C for 24 h. Following this step, the system was allowed to cool to room temperature (∼25 °C), and the resulting aqueous solution underwent dialysis for 24 h, with deionized water changes every 4 h. Finally, a yellowish aqueous solution containing the carbon dots was obtained and referred to as QDs. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of this solution was measured to be 4.5 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of crosslinked β-CD and β-CD(CA)/QDs with citric acid
ce:section.ce:para: Crosslinked β-CD was synthesized by polycondensation of citric acid (CA) with β-CD, using KH2PO4 as a catalyst (Li et al., 2019). In brief, the following procedure was used: CA (0.5 g, 2.6 mmol), β-CD (1.0 g, 0.9 mmol), KH2PO4 (0.25 g, 1.4 mmol), and 25 mL of deionized water were mixed in a round bottom flask and stirred in a water bath at 25 °C. The mixture was transferred to a Petri dish and heated in an oven (140 °C) for 3.5 h. After cooling, the crude product was purified by soaking and washing with deionized water several times, filtered, and vacuum-dried at 50 °C overnight. The obtained material is labeled as β-CD(CA). For the preparation of QDs-containing β-cyclodextrin (β-CD(CA)/QDs,) the same process was reproduced by replacing the solvent, deionized water, with the QDs solution, absolute fluorescence quantum yield of the fluorescent material obtained was 3.7 %. The experimental sequences used in the preparation of both materials are summarized in Fig. 1 .

For the swelling process, the previously weighed adsorbents (m d, ca. 30 mg) were placed in an Erlenmeyer flask and filled with 30 mL of distilled water at room temperature. After 16 h, the swollen adsorbents were removed from the Erlenmeyer, the excess surface water was wiped off and the weight of the adsorbent (m s) was measured. The swelling degree, H
p , was calculated by using Eq. (1).

PEG-400, QDs solution, β-CD, β-CD(CA), and β-CD(CA)/QDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra with coupled ATR module were recorded on a Shimadzu IR-Affinity-1 spectrometer (Japan), in a wavelength interval of 400–4000 cm−1. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Netzsch Instruments, model TG209–F3, Germany) were performed using 3 mg of each sample in a temperature range from 25 to 500 °C, a heating rate of 10 °C/min, and under a 50 mL/min flow rate of nitrogen. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with Energy Dispersive X-Ray (EDX) analyzer (Gemini 2-Zeiss, Merlin-Zeiss, Germany) was utilized to examine the morphology. SEM images were taken at 10.0 kV and x100 or x10000 of magnification. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired in a JEOL JEM 2100 microscope (USA) at 200 kV. Samples were previously dispersed in water and then placed into the analyzing grids (UC-A on holey 400 mesh Cu grids, Ted Pella ref. 01824) by drop-casting, followed by drying at room temperature. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were conducted using 1 mL of QDs solutions, β-CD(CA), and β-CD(CA)/QDs dispersion in a 0.01 mmol/L KCl solution at 25 °C (Zetasizer NanoZS, Malvern, USA). ζ-Potential measurements were carried out using the same solutions via aqueous electrophoresis measurements, and the values were calculated from electrophoretic mobility using the Smoluchowski relationship (Zetasizer NanoZS, Malvern, USA).

Batch experiments were carried out to compare the ability of β-CD(CA) and β-CD(CA)/QDs to sorb Cd, Cu, Ni, and Pb divalent ions. For the preliminary adsorption experiments, an amount of adsorbent (5 mg) was placed in an aqueous solution (5 mL) of a metal ion salt (100 mg/L). The solution was kept stirring, at 150 rpm, for 24 h. Then, the solution was filtered using a Nylon 0.22 μm filter (LabFil).

To compare the precursor and the polymeric matrix with and without QDs, different adsorption parameters have been evaluated: solid-liquid ratio (0.3–3.7 mg), initial metals concentration (1–15 mg/L), and pH solution (2.3–4.8). For the latter experiments, the pH of the solution was adjusted by adding HNO3 (0.1 mol/L) solution.

The optimal experimental conditions for the fluorescent material β-CD(CA)/QDs were investigated using a central composite design (CCDR), 23 × 2 with 3 central points and 6 axial points, totaling 31 experiments. Three independent variables were evaluated: contact time between adsorbent and adsorbate (1–24 h), adsorbent dose (1–20 mg), and solution pH (2–5). The variables and levels used in the factorial design are presented in Table 1 . The experiments were performed in triplicate according to the Statistica© software (StatSoft Inc., USA) with a significance level of 95 %. Other experimental parameters (metal species concentration (50 mg/L), temperature (25 °C), solution volume (5 mL), and stirring rate (100 rpm)) were kept fixed.

The adsorption performance of the materials towards Cd(II), Cu(II), Ni(II), and Pb(II) ions was evaluated by calculating the removal efficiency (%) and adsorption capacity (mg/g) values using Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively:

The q
e and removal (%) values obtained from the factorial design investigation were subjected to mathematical treatment to determine desirability, which identifies the conditions that maximize qe without experiencing a significant reduction in removal efficiency. The desirability data were analyzed using the desirability method proposed by Derringer and Suich (Derringer & Suich, 1980; D'Hondt et al., 2014), and it is estimated using Eq. (4):

To evaluate the adsorption selectivity of β-CD(CA)/QDs towards the tested metal ions, a quaternary (multicomponent) solution of Cd(II), Cu(II), Ni(II), and Pb(II) (50 mL, 25 mg/L of each compound) was prepared and added to a 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask. Then, 30 mg of material was added to the flask, which was kept under orbital stirring (180 rpm) at room temperature for 16 h (equilibrium time). The metal ion quantification has been conducted as described before. The selectivity parameter was calculated per Eq. (7):
(7)
Selectivity =
m i

where m i, m ii, …, m
n refer to the mass of each adsorbed compound. This experiment was also done in triplicate.

The recycling of the post-utilized adsorbents was done by desorbing the metal ions (Cd(II), Cu(II), Ni(II), and Pb(II)). For such, just after the adsorption experiments the polymers were recovered and immersed in a HNO3 solution (pH 2, 15 mL), which was kept under orbital stirring (100 rpm) for 30 min at room temperature. The metal ion quantification is like that previously described. The desorption efficiency was calculated by using Eq. (8):
(8)
Desorption
%
=
q des
q ads
× 100
where q des and q ads are the amount of compound desorbed and adsorbed by the adsorbent, respectively. For the reuse experiments, the recycled adsorbents were employed in new adsorption/desorption cycles. In total, 10 consecutive cycles were conducted, maintaining the same adsorption and desorption conditions as described previously.

Batch experiments were conducted to evaluate the ability of the β-CD(CA)/QDs materials to detect Cd(II), Cu(II), Ni(II), and Pb(II) ions. In these experiments, the adsorbent was brought into contact with solutions containing different concentrations of the respective metal ions. The stability of the materials was continuously monitored by fluorescence measurements over a period of 60 days. In addition, selectivity detection tests were performed by immersing the β-CD(CA)/QDs materials in solutions containing all four metal species: Cd(II), Cu(II), Ni(II), and Pb(II).

All fluorescence measurements were carried out using a Perkin Elmer LS 45 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a plotter unit and a 1 cm quartz cell. Absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-2600i spectrophotometer (Japan). Fluorescence decay measurements were conducted using a custom-built nanosecond time-correlated single photon counting (ns-TCSPC) apparatus, as described in Seixas de Melo et al. (2013). The deconvolution of fluorescence decay curves was performed using a modulation function method, as implemented by G. Striker in the SAND software, which has been previously reported in the literature (Striker et al., 1999).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of β-CD(CA) and β-CD(CA)/QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1 shows the FTIR spectra of the QDs precursor, PEG-400, and of the obtained QDs. For PEG, it is possible to observe characteristic bands at 3449 cm−1, 2846 cm−1, and 1074 cm−1 attributed to the stretching of the OH bonds, stretching of the methyl CH bonds, and symmetrical elongation of the C–O–C bonds, respectively (Xiao et al., 2017). Concerning QDs, it is worth noticing an increase in the intensity of the band at 3449 cm−1 related to the increase of OH bonds of hydroxyl groups, as these tend to reach the surface during the passivation of QDs (Xiao et al., 2017). On the other hand, the band at 2846 cm−1, assigned to stretching of the CH bond, shows a decrease in the intensity which might be justified by a more stressed higher energy chemical bond (Mohamed et al., 2017). Compared to its precursor, the quantum dot solution presents two new bands at 1718 cm−1 and 1687 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of the CO bond and vibration of the CC bond, respectively; these new bands appear also due to the passivation process of the surface, the main indicator of the formation of QDs (Fan et al., 2014).

FTIR spectra of β-CD, β-CD(CA), and β-CD(CA)/QDs are shown in Fig. 2 . In the β-CD spectrum, it is possible to observe specific peaks at 3270 cm−1 assigned to OH bonds, at 2924 cm−1 indicating the vibration of asymmetric CH2 stretching, and at 1024 cm−1 referring to glycosidic bonds of the cyclodextrin (Chen et al., 2014). For β-CD(CA), bands appeared at 1728 cm−1 (vibration of CO bonds), 1074 cm−1, and 1024 cm−1 indicating the formation of ester groups through stretching of C–O–C bonds (Junthip, 2019). This is justified by the reaction of citric acid with the hydroxyl groups of cyclodextrin through a nucleophilic acyl substitution (Hafiz Rozaini et al., 2022). In addition, the band referring to hydroxyl groups undergoes a significant shift to 3329 cm−1, which can be due to unreacted hydroxyl groups of the β-CD and hydroxyl groups of CA, thus indicating the formation of the water-insoluble β-CD(CA). Finally, the β-CD(CA)/QDs spectrum showed β-CD absorption bands representative of asymmetric glycosidic stretching vibrations (C–O–C) at 1150 cm−1 and bending vibrations of CH–OH bonds (at 2942 cm−1) demonstrating the presence of QDs into β-CD. The occurrence of abundant hydroxyl groups in the β-CD facilitates the self-assembly interaction mechanism with quantum dots generating strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds.

Thermal analysis was conducted to gain further insights into the thermal stability of the synthesized materials. Fig. 3(A) displays the TGA curve of β-CD, revealing two distinct weight loss steps. The first, occurring in the temperature range of 30–101 °C, corresponds to the loss of adsorbed water. The second weight loss step, accounting for approximately 72 % of the total weight loss, took place between 210 and 350 °C, with a peak degradation temperature at 309 °C, attributed to the thermal degradation of β-CD (Trotta et al., 2000). The thermogram for crosslinked β-cyclodextrin (β-CD(CA)) closely resembled that of raw β-CD. However, it exhibited a narrower temperature range (290–330 °C) for the second weight loss step, and the maximum degradation temperature shifted to 323 °C, indicating a more ordered crosslinked structure.

In the presence of QDs, the TGA curve of β-CD(CA)/QDs exhibited slight changes. Aside from the weight loss attributed to water evaporation, a new weight loss step with a maximum temperature of approximately 260 °C was observed (Fig. 3(B)). This additional weight loss could be attributed to the non-reactive portions of PEG-400 used in the crosslinking process (Gao et al., 2014). The third weight loss step, related to the thermal degradation of the β-CD(CA) network, exhibited a lower maximum temperature compared to the material without QDs. This suggests that the presence of QDs may affect the crosslinking of β-CD by CA, leading to a reduction in the thermal stability of β-CD(CA)/QDs. Furthermore, the thermogram of β-CD(CA)/QDs indicated a higher residual weight at 500 °C, likely due to the presence of QDs in this material.

TEM microscopy was employed to confirm the presence of QDs in the β-CD(CA)/QDs adsorbent, as shown in Fig. 4 . TEM images of β-CD(CA)/QDs reveal the abundant presence of spherical nanoparticles with a diameter size smaller than 10 nm. High-resolution TEM measurements further revealed well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm (inset in Fig. 4B), corresponding to the (100) diffraction planes of graphene (Cao et al., 2022). As expected, TEM analysis of β-CD and β-CD(CA) did not reveal the presence of any spherical nanoparticles or highly ordered structures. SEM microscopy was also employed to assess the effect of QDs on the morphology of β-CD(CA), as shown in Fig. 5 . Regarding the morphology and size distribution of both materials, it is noteworthy that β-CD(CA) exhibits asymmetrically sized agglomerated particles with a wide size distribution (5.8 ± 2.7 μm), as determined by DLS measurements. Additionally, the dense particles display an amorphous and irregular surface (Fig. 5A and B). In contrast, β-CD(CA)/QDs, while also displaying an amorphous and irregular morphology (Fig. 5A and D), feature significantly more homogeneous sizing (3.5 ± 1.6 μm) according to DLS data, with particles that are more dispersed. The organization of the materials can be influenced by the dispersion of quantum dots within the β-CD lattice structure (Xu & Li, 2018). Considering the interaction of QDs with β-CD, it can be inferred that QDs influence the amorphic nature of the material and reduce the size of the dispersed particles on the surface. These characteristics are beneficial for the adsorption process, as will be discussed later.

Table 2 presents the swelling degree of both materials. Notably, the presence of QDs results in a 35 % increase in the H
p , rising from 363 ± 7 % to 491 ± 3 %. This difference in the swelling degree can be attributed to the higher content of hydroxyl groups on the surface of the adsorbents due to the presence of QDs. This increase in hydroxyl groups enhances the active sites available for hydrogen bonding, thereby facilitating the entry of molecules into the pores of the material (Silva et al., 2023). In terms of morphology, materials containing QDs can exhibit a greater dispersion of these nanomaterials on their surface, which further facilitates the swelling process.

The ζ-potential measurements revealed that both QDs nanoparticles and β-CD(CA) have a negatively charged surface at a fixed pH (pH 6.5), which can be attributed to deprotonated carboxylic groups. The combination of β-CD(CA) and QDs also resulted in a material with a negatively charged surface (−46 ± 7 mV), although this value decreased compared to β-CD(CA). As mentioned earlier, the entrapment of QDs may affect the crosslinking of β-CD with CA, potentially reducing the number of ionizable carboxylic groups. However, like QDs and β-CD(CA), the β-CD(CA)/QDs adsorbent exhibited a ζ-potential value high enough to prevent particle interactions, promoting their dispersion in the aqueous medium.

In addition to ζ-potential measurements, pHPZC values were also determined. The pHPZC value corresponds to the pH at which the [H+] and [OH−] concentrations on the surface of the materials are equal. In general, pH conditions lower than the pHPZC indicate that [H+] > [OH−], while pH higher than the pHPZC means that [H+] < [OH−]. It is observed that the entrapment of QDs into β-CD(CA) shifted the pHPZC from 5.8 to 3.6 (Table 2), indicating that the pH range in which the β-CD(CA)/QDs adsorbent exhibits an excess of negative charges on its surface is wider compared to the adsorbent without QDs. This trend can be associated with the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the QDs, which appear to be preferentially distributed on the surface of β-CD(CA)/QDs (Cunha et al., 2018).

This section serves two distinct objectives: first, to assess the capabilities of the prepared materials as adsorbents, and second, to evaluate their performance as a fluorescent probe, as these two processes are interconnected. To achieve these goals, various experiments were conducted to gather pertinent chemical information regarding the adsorption process.

As a preliminary experiment, the influence of counterions on the adsorption process of divalent metal ions (Ni(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II)) was conducted, and the results are presented in Fig. S2 (Supporting information). It is evident that the adsorption of metal ions depends on the choice of counterions, with nitrate salts showing a favorable effect on adsorption. The adsorption capacities (q
e ) for Ni(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II) with nitrate salts were found to be 50, 47, 35, and 32 mg/g, respectively, for the β-CD(CA)/QDs adsorbent. This trend can be attributed to various factors, including the solubility of these salts in water, where NO3 − has a solubility of 874 g/L, followed by CH3COO− with 365 g/L, and SO4 2− with 4.27 g/L. Other properties, such as the mobility and availability of these species in solution, may also influence the adsorption process (Matias et al., 2023). Furthermore, the β-CD(CA)/QDs material exhibited higher adsorption capacities compared to β-CD(CA) alone, with a 16 % higher capacity for Ni(II) adsorption and a remarkable 40 % improvement in adsorption of Cu(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II). It's worth noting that tests conducted in the presence of saline solutions, such as NaCl, did not result in significant changes in adsorption capacity. The variations observed did not exceed a range of 2 % loss or gain in the adsorption capacity of both synthesized materials.

Given that the β-CD(CA)/QDs material emerged as the superior adsorbent, capable of fulfilling the requirements of both applications in this study and considering the chosen counterions for the evaluated species, we proceeded with a factorial design to establish the optimal pH conditions, adsorption time, and adsorbent mass to be used in fixed-concentration solutions, as described in the experimental section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the optimal experimental conditions for adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: From the experimental results, a Pareto graph was generated and is presented in Fig. S3A. The Pareto chart, obtained through experimental planning of the DCCR type, provides insights into how each variable influences the system. The most significant variable in the system is the adsorbent dosage (denoted by letter A in Fig. S3A), demonstrating a negative effect. In other words, an increase in the mass of the adsorbent leads to a decrease in qe, consistent with (Eq. (3)). The pH of the metal solution exhibited a significant positive effect, as did the variable contact time (C.T.), ranking as the second and third most relevant variables, respectively. Higher pH values resulted in better adsorption performance, which can be attributed to factors such as ζ-potential and pHPZC. The adsorbent tested has a negatively charged surface for pH values above 3.65, favoring the adsorption of positively charged ions. Among the interactions between variables, the 2 × 3 interaction (adsorbent dose × pH) stood out as having a significant negative effect, aligning with the individual results observed for these variables. The quadratic effects also demonstrated significance, though to a lesser extent.

Focusing on the significant terms, the analysis of variance (ANOVA) for the overall adsorption of ions in β-CD(CA)/QDs is summarized in Table S1 (Supporting information). The obtained F values confirmed that the models were meaningful and suitable for generating response surfaces, exploring the main effects of independent factors and their interactions in the removal of metal ions (Fakhri, 2014). Accordingly, the response surfaces revealed that the optimal pH for the removal of Ni(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II) is 4.25. At this pH, metal ions do not undergo hydrolysis (Baes & Mesmer, 1976), while the carboxyl groups of the adsorbent are deprotonated. These carboxyl groups have a pKa between 3.2 and 4.0, suggesting an enhanced electrostatic interaction between divalent metal ions and carboxylate groups of β-CD(CA)/QDs at this pH level. For alkaline pH values (pH ≥ 6.0), adsorption may be hindered due to the precipitation of metal hydroxides. Additionally, the response surfaces (Fig. S3(B-D)) indicated that the ideal amount of adsorbent for removing the metals is 5.0 mg. Consequently, the optimized analytical conditions employed in the subsequent experiments were as follows: 16 h of contact time, 5.0 mg of adsorbent, and a pH of the metal solution set to 4.3.

Adsorption experiments were conducted to investigate the adsorption of metal ions on β-CD-based adsorbents, using the optimized conditions revealed by CCD analysis. Calculated q
t values and removal efficiencies are shown in Fig. 6 , which depicts the sorption kinetics for the removal of metal ions by β-CD(C) and β-CD/QDs. The q
t values increased rapidly in the first 170 min of contact for all tested metals. Above 750 min, the q
t values tend to attain equilibrium. Overall, the maximum removal efficiencies of Ni(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II) ions onto β-CD(CA)/QDs were 96 %, 91 %, 68 %, and 58 %. Thus, the adsorption capacities and removal efficiencies followed the order Ni(II) > Cu(II) > Cd(II) > Pb(II). The negatively charged sites on the surface of the adsorbents attract metal ions, and the effective binding of these metals to the β-CD(CA)/QDs sites is influenced by adsorbate properties, particularly the ionic radius, which affects the mobility of these ions in the solution (Balasooriya et al., 2022). Among the metal ions studied, Ni(II) ions have the smallest ionic radius (0.83 Å), followed by Cu(II) (0.87 Å), Cd(II) (1.09 Å), and Pb(II) (1.33 Å). Consequently, Ni(II) ions are adsorbed more readily than the other metallic ions due to their smaller ionic radius, facilitating their interaction with the surface and internal cavities of the adsorbent (Persson, 2010).

The adsorption kinetics of the tested metal ions on β-CD(CA) and β-CD(CA)/QDs were examined using two simplified models: the pseudo-first order (Eq. (9)) and pseudo-second order (Eq. (10)) kinetic equations, which are given below:
(9)

where k 1 (1/min) and k 2 (g/mg min) are the rate constants of the applied kinetic models and q e (mg/g) is the amount of metal adsorbed per gram of adsorbent under experimental conditions (Jing et al., 2023). The kinetic parameters calculated from the pseudo-first order and pseudo-second order plots are listed in Table 3 and Figs. S4 and S5.

The robustness of such models was analyzed using the coefficient of determination (R2) and the Akaike information criterion (AIC) (Matias et al., 2023). The AIC measures the prediction error, and the smaller its value, the better the fit. It can be determined by Eq. (11):
(11)
AIC = n × log
s 2
n
+ 2 K
where n is the number of experimental points, s 2 is the residual sum of squares and K is the number of model independent parameters. This test allows evaluation of the agreement between experimental and theoretical data and thus determines which kinetic model adequately describes the adsorption process. From the analysis of both R2 and AIC values, reported in Table 3, the sorption kinetic of all metal ions on CD-containing materials follows the pseudo-second order model. It is also observed that the k 2 rate constant changes in the following order: k 2 (Ni(II)) > k 2 (Cu(II)) > k 2 (Cd(II)) > k 2 (Pb(II)).

Additionally, the adsorption mechanism of Ni(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II) divalent ions on β-CD(CA) and β-CD(CA)/QDs adsorbents was further investigated by sorption isotherms (Fig. S6). Herein, two isotherm models were utilized to fit the experimental data: Langmuir (Eq. (12)) and Langmuir-Freundlich (Eq. (13)) equations (Ngo et al., 2023), which are described as:
(12)

where K L (L/mg) K LF (L/mg) are the Langmuir and the Langmuir–Freundlich constants and b is the Langmuir–Freundlich heterogeneity constant. The analysis of these equations reveals that Eq. (12) is a particular case of Eq. (13) when b = 1. However, from a phenomenological point of view, while the Langmuir equation is valid for a homogeneous interaction surface where a monolayer of the adsorbate is obtained, the Langmuir-Freundlich isotherm suggests the occurrence of a multilayer in a heterogenous surface (Vareda et al., 2016). The computed fitting parameters of the isotherms are summarized in Table 4 .

Desorption experiments were conducted to investigate the metal-releasing capacity of the metal-loaded β-CD(CA)/QDs adsorbent and its potential for reuse in new adsorption/desorption cycles. The ability to reuse the adsorbent is essential for practical and cost-effective applications. In these experiments, the adsorption sites on the surface of the granules were regenerated using an HNO3 solution (pH ∼2). The protonation of the functional groups on the surface of the adsorbent caused by HNO3 breaks their interactions with the adsorbed metals, releasing them into the eluent. Following the regeneration treatment, the adsorbents were reused in nine additional adsorption/desorption cycles, totalling 10 cycles, as illustrated in Fig. 7 .

The removal profiles for each cycle followed the same order observed during the adsorption process: Ni(II) > Cu(II) > Cd(II) > Pb(II). There was a loss of 22 % for Ni(II) adsorption, 27 % for Cu(II) adsorption, 44 % for Cd(II), and 48 % for Pb(II). These findings suggest that the β-CD(CA)/QDs adsorbent exhibits remarkable reusability in multiple cycles, making it a promising choice for practical adsorption applications. The efficiency of ion desorption was influenced by the characteristics previously mentioned, where ions with smaller ionic radii had a higher diffusion rate out of the adsorbent (Kim et al., 2022). These results encourage the use of this material as an adsorbent in wastewater treatment for metal removal and warrant further investigation in sensing applications, as discussed in the following sections.

The ability of β-CD(CA)/QDs to selectively adsorb the studied metals was investigated using a mixed solution containing the same concentration of each metal, as detailed in the experimental section. The results, presented in Fig. S8, demonstrate that Ni(II) exhibited the highest q
e value (20 mg/g), followed by Cu(II) (17 mg/g), Cd(II) (14 mg/g), and Pb(II) (9 mg/g), respectively. Similarly, the selectivity parameters followed the same trend [Ni(II) 0.34 > Cu(II) 0.28 > Cd(II) 0.23 > Pb(II) 0.16]. Based on these results, there is an indication that β-CD(CA)/QDs can selectively adsorb metals according to their charge density. In fact, the hydrated radius (r
h ) of these metal ions follows the order r
h (Ni(II)) < r
h (Cu(II)) < r
h (Cd(II)) < r
h (Pb(II)) (Persson, 2010). This suggests that the r
h of the metal ions follow the same order. This is also in agreement with the hypothesis that the main interactions between the composite and the ions are of an electrostatic nature.

Furthermore, when compared with the experiments conducted with single metal solutions, the adsorption capacities reported in Fig. S8 were lower, suggesting that the metals can compete for the active sites of the adsorbent. In a solution containing a mixture of metal ions, the adsorption process is influenced by a combination of effects, in which the ionic radius plays a crucial role in determining the interaction of metals with the adsorbents (Bohli et al., 2017).

The optical properties of the β-CD(CA)/QDs material were investigated using UV–Vis spectroscopy and photoluminescence. As shown in Fig. S9(A), the absorption spectrum of β-CD(CA)/QDs displays a broad absorption band with a maximum at 215 nm and a shoulder at around 268 nm, which, as elsewhere reported are assigned to π–π* and n–π* transitions, respectively, of the QDs trapped in the polymeric matrix (Q. Li et al., 2021). The fluorescence emission spectra of the β-CD(CA)/QDs collected at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. S9(B). Excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior was observed for the investigated β-CD(CA)/QD and assigned to the presence of different particle sizes (quantum confinement effect) (Gan et al., 2016) or to the entrapment of these QDs in β-CD. Indeed, the fluorescence decay of the β-CD(CA)/QDs sample was found to be well fitted with a biexponential decay law with lifetimes of 2.0 ns and 6.9 ns (Fig. S9(C)), values longer than those previously reported (Kar et al., 2023). This behavior is attributed to the crystalline organization of the prepared β-CD(CA)/QDs and reduction of the QDs surface trap-states (and concomitant enhancement of the excited-state radiative decay) promoted by the interaction of the β-CD with the surface charges of these CDs (Mishra et al., 2021). The TEM image in Fig. 4 supports the presence of spherical particles with monodisperse structures and a size diameter lower than 10 nm for the investigated β-CD(CA)/QDs. Moreover, with the HRTEM images (Fig. 4), it is possible to confirm the crystalline character of the QDs nanoparticles synthesized and entrapped in the β-CD(CA) matrix (Ge et al., 2020).

Before the fluorescence quenching studies the emission stability of the β-CD(CA)/QDs was evaluated under operable conditions of pH (2–6), temperature (10–50 °C), and material storage time (0–28 days). The pH-dependent studies showed that the emission intensity of β-CD(CA)/QDs is constant until pH 5, and a small decrease is observed at pH 6 due to the reduced degree of protonation of the OH groups of the QDs, see Fig. S10(A). The fluorescent intensity was also found stable when varying temperature conditions (10–50 °C), as shown in Fig. S10(B). Furthermore, the synthesized β-CD(CA)/QDs probes showed excellent photostability over the days, (Fig. S10(C)) as their emission intensity did not change after 28 consecutive days of experiment with daily excitation for 1 h with a UV lamp. Worth mentioning that the stability found in β-CD(CA)/QDs materials is not common in works that use only QDs in solution (Ahirwar et al., 2023). Thus the higher stability can be assigned to the anchoring of the QDs in the β-CD, which protects the surface of the QDs from damage.

To understand the detection capacity of the β-CD/QDs probe, its fluorescence response was measured at various Ni(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II) concentrations while maintaining the best experimental conditions (pH 4.3 and 25 °C). As shown in Fig. 8 (A-D), increasing ion concentrations produced a quenching in the fluorescence of the β-CD/QDs probe(Biranje et al., 2021). Fig. 8E shows the fluorescence emission spectra of solutions containing a mixture of metal ions. The results indicate that the presence of Ni(II) enhances the quenching effect, and the successive order of detection follows this sequence: Ni(II) > Cu(II) > Cd(II) > Pb(II).

In general, the Stern-Volmer plots for quenching β-CD(CA)/QDs show clear downward curvature, suggesting the presence of two fluorophore populations with one of them not being accessible to the quencher (Fig. 9(A)) (Lakowicz, 2006; Mátyus et al., 2006). The good linear regression obtained from the Lehrer modified Stern-Volmer plot, Eq. (14), shows straightforward evidence for a shielded fraction of β-CD(CA)/QDs (Fig. 9B), which are inaccessible to the metal quenchers:
(14)

where F 0 is the fluorescence observed in the absence of a quencher, F is the fluorescence observed in the presence of a quencher, f
a is the fraction of the initial fluorescence that is accessible to the quencher, K
SVa is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant of the accessible fraction, and [Q] is the concentration of quencher. For the investigated metal ions, the accessible Stern-Volmer constant, K
SVa , was found to be in the 3777–13,359 M−1 range, and the f
a values lower than 1, show that the metals ions did not access all the β-CD(CA)/QDs (see Fig. 9B). Worth informing the reader that the calculated f
a values decrease following the same order found for Ni(II) > Cu(II) > Cd(II) > Pb(II) adsorption capacity, thus highlighting the potential of the developed polymeric β-CD(CA)/QDs as materials for sorption/removal and fluorescence sensing of Ni(II) metal ions from wastewaters. The dissociation constants, K d, were also calculated from Eq. (14) (as the inverse of f
a .K
SVa ), with values ranging from 1.66 × 10−4 to 5.52 × 10−4 M. The binding constant, log K a, obtained by using the double-logarithmic plot (log(F 0-F)/F vs log [Q]), see Fig. 9C, provides further support for the higher affinity of the β-CD(CA)/QDs towards Ni(II) ions (log K
d = 1.77), corroborating the adsorption data. Based on this data, the limit of detection (LOD) values have been calculated, and they are as follows: LOD(Ni(II)) = 5.1 ± 0.3 ppm, LOD(Cu(II)) = 3.6 ± 0.2 ppm, LOD(Cd(II)) = 6.4 ± 0.5 ppm, and LOD(Pb(II)) = 2.3 ± 0.1 ppm. These LOD values are in line with those reported in the literature for similar materials used in the sensing of these metal ions (Baragau et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2021).

In contrast to organic contaminants and biocontaminants, heavy metal ions are highly soluble and do not readily undergo natural degradation. This manuscript focuses on the development of a material designed to efficiently remove divalent metal ions. While some of these metals are essential trace elements for organisms, excessive absorption can lead to biological dysfunctions, emphasizing the significance of their detection and removal for both environmental monitoring and human health protection.

In practical applications, modern pollutant remediation systems demand simultaneous and rapid detection of analytes to enable real-time assessments of operational efficiency. This is particularly crucial for on-site monitoring of material flows and water treatment processes. Consequently, porous materials with high sensitivity and exceptional adsorption capacity can be tailored to address various types of contaminants. The β-CD(CA)/QDs material showcased in this research is proficient at adsorbing all the metallic species studied, exhibiting distinct levels of fluorescent responses for each species based on the observed degree of selectivity, as demonstrated in Fig. 8.

In summary, we employed a one-step β-CD crosslinking method, utilizing citric acid as the crosslinker, to encapsulate QDs nanocrystals within its structure. The resulting β-CD(CA)/QDs material demonstrated dual functionality, enabling both adsorption/removal and fluorescence detection of Ni(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II) species in aqueous environments. This material exhibited many advantageous features, including high optical transmittance, excellent mechanical robustness, and thermal and photoluminescent stability. Thanks to its remarkable photoluminescence, the sensor displayed heightened sensitivity and capability for detecting metal fluorescence. Additionally, the porous structure of the detection probe facilitated the simultaneous adsorption of metals, making it suitable as a filter for rapid decontamination and efficient removal of metal ion pollution. Notably, this process exhibited certain selectivity concerning metal removal, and the adsorbent could be reused for at least 10 consecutive runs. This study opens avenues for future optimizations, such as real sample detection and fine-tuning the proportion of QDs within the polymer structure, among other potential advancements.
